sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: UP.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 38, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: - **Blocks**: Orange (2 rows) + blue (3 rows) = 5×5 block, currently at rows 40-44, cols 49-53 (rightmost) - **Movement**: LEFT/RIGHT moves blocks ±5 cols (4 positions: 34-38, 39-43, 44-48, 49-53). UP moves blocks up 5 rows. DOWN only affects timer. - **Corridor**: L-shaped path in charcoal: vertical (cols 29-33, rows 30-39) + horizontal (cols 24-33, rows 40-44) - **White cross**: Stationary at rows 31-33, cols 20-22 - **Goal**: Navigate blocks through the corridor to match/align with the white cross target
- Plan: Move blocks LEFT to corridor entrance, then UP into vertical corridor, then further LEFT/UP toward the white cross. Need to test if blocks can enter the corridor.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 37 actions; a strong score needs about 22 or fewer. You are 1.7x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The blocks are still at rows 25-29, cols 29-33 (orange at 25-26, blue at 27-29). The UP action didn't move the blocks, which means they can't go higher than rows 25-29. So rows 25-29 is the highest position.
Wait, looking at the result, the blocks didn't move. The orange is still at [[25, 29], [25, 33], [26, 33], [26, 29]] and blue is at [[27, 29], [27, 33], [29, 33], [29, 29]]. So the blocks are still at rows 25-29, cols 29-33.
So the blocks can't move UP from rows 25-29. This means rows 25-29 is the highest position.
Now I need to figure out the goal. The blocks are at rows 25-29, cols 29-33. The white cross is at rows 31-32, cols 21-22. The blocks can move LEFT and RIGHT, but not UP (already at highest) or DOWN (only affects timer).
Let me think about this. The blocks can be at:
- Rows: 25-29 (highest), 30-34, 35-39, etc. (but can't move DOWN)
- Cols: various positions
Wait, but earlier I moved the blocks UP from rows 30-34 to rows 25-29. And now I can't move UP further. So the blocks are at the highest position.
But the blocks can still move LEFT and RIGHT. Let me try moving the blocks LEFT to see if they can reach the white cross's column.
The white cross is at cols 21-22. The blocks are at cols 29-33. If I move LEFT, the blocks go to cols 24-28. If I move LEFT again, the blocks go to cols 19-23, which overlaps with the cross at cols 21-22.
But I already tried rows 25-29, cols 19-23 and the level didn't complete. And I also tried rows 30-34, cols 19-23 and the level didn't complete.
Hmm, maybe the goal is not about the white cross at all. Let me look at the image again.
Looking at the image, I see:
1. Gray corridor structure
2. Orange/blue blocks at the top of the vertical corridor
3. White cross to the left of the blocks
4. Blue icon at the top (in black square) at rows 11-13, cols 35-37
5. Blue L-shaped icon at bottom-left (in black square)
6. Timer bar at bottom
The blue icon at the top is at cols 35-37, which is to the right of the blocks at cols 29-33. Maybe the goal is to have the blocks aligned with the blue icon at the top.
Let me try moving the blocks RIGHT to cols 34-38, which would align them with the blue icon at cols 35-37.
Actually, wait. The blue icon at the top is at cols 35-37, and the blocks are at cols 29-33. If I move RIGHT, the blocks go to cols 34-38. The blue icon is at cols 35-37, which is inside the blocks' column range. So the blocks would overlap horizontally with the blue icon.
But the blue icon is at rows 11-13, and the blocks are at rows 25-29. They're far apart vertically. So the blocks can't overlap with the blue icon.
Unless... the goal is to have the blocks at a specific column position, aligned with the blue icon. Let me try moving RIGHT to cols 34-38.
Actually, I realize I should check if the blocks can move RIGHT from cols 29-33. The corridor might have walls that prevent movement.
Let me try RIGHT and see what happens.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Blocks at rows 25-29, cols 29-33
# Can't move UP (already at highest)
# Can't move DOWN (only affects timer)
# Try RIGHT to cols 34-38, aligning with blue icon at cols 35-37
action(['RIGHT'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
